data.title: The Drive For Profits Is Missing The Green At FST Corp.
data.description:
Few stock stories from 2025 are more unique than that of FST Corp. (NASDAQ: KBSX). The Taiwanese company is a producer of golf equipment that has been in business for almost 50 years. Yet, anyone looking for stock prices for FST will not see anything prior to January 16 of this year. On that date, FST was listed in the US through a reverse merger with a SPAC, Chenghe Acquisition I Company.

SPACs are rarely associated with sports equipment makers. Their targets are much more likely to be involved in industries like electric vehicles, fintech, or biotechnology. True to form, Chenghe had initially announced that it was aiming to leverage its expertise and resources in industries related to green energy, biotechnology, or optoelectronics in the Asian-Pacific region.

The fact that Chenghe ended up rolling over its business into a golf equipment manufacturer may seem like a stretch, but FST could be considered a technology company, even if the purpose of the technology is to produce sports equipment. FST also offered Chenghe the opportunity to tap into a premium niche market in the US. Most SPACs have 18 to 24 months to complete a deal before funding is returned to investors. Since Chenghe's SPAC was formed in October 2023, the clock was ticking on its existence.

To say that the debut of the stock for the new corporation has been underwhelming is an understatement. FST has lost almost 80% of its value since its IPO. After opening on January 16 at $12.30 per share, the stock hit a high of $15.48 before closing at $7.90. From there, the stock sputtered in neutral for about a month before diving into penny stock territory. At the time of my writing this article, the stock is currently trading at under $1.80 per share.

Has the market overreacted because the words "SPAC" and "golf" are just not seen as a natural combination? Is there something else happening here that is causing the stock to crater at such a rapid rate after its IPO? After reading information about the company and its history, and looking at the company's outlook for the remainder of this year, I am not ready to call this a bargain, even at its depressed price. The company is losing money and burning through cash at a rapid rate. I believe the stock is worth holding only for investors who have a very high tolerance for risk.

Company Snapshot

Femco Steel Technology Corporation, or FST Corp, develops, manufactures, and sells steel and graphite golf shafts under the KBS brand. The company produces not only its own branded products but also OEM products for resale. The company is based in Chiayi, Taiwan, and was established in 1976. The company did not enter the golf equipment business until 1992 and did not begin selling to US consumers until 2008.

In the last two years, FST has opened two KBS Golf Experience stores - one in Carlsbad, California, and the other in Tokyo, Japan - in an effort to increase brand awareness and connect with customers on a more personal level. The company is currently looking into options to build similar stores in China, South Korea, and the Middle East. FST also operates "Hit Cat Kitchen", a restaurant in Chiayi, Taiwan, and "Hit Cat Brewing", a bar in Taipei. Both sites are decorated with KBS merchandise and located strategically near popular golf courses.

The company released its 2024 financial report on May 16, 2025. For the full year, FST reported revenues of $36.5 million, a 27% increase over the previous year. Almost 97% of that revenue comes from the sale of golf shafts. The operations from the retail locations and restaurants provides an additional $936 million in revenue. The company also started a software service providing analytical data in 2024. This revenue stream contributed less than 1% to the company's top line.

The stock for the company currently trades at a market cap of $113.8 million. FST Corp pays no dividend.

Company Analysis

Even though revenues were up last year, the company reported a loss of $3.24 million, or $0.09 per share, for FY 2024. This was also a larger number from 2023, when the company only lost $2.17 million, or $0.05 per share. Just over a third of the company's losses in 2024, $1.2 million, was attributed to income tax expenses.

The full explanation from the company's results in FY 2024 can be seen below. While revenues were up significantly, cost of sales and operating expenses grew faster. If FST was a true startup that had only been in business for a couple of years, some of this would be easier to explain, but these numbers are confounding for a company that was profitable as recently as 2022.

The 28.9% jump in General and Administrative Expenses shown below is especially troublesome, but the company attributed some of the increase to costs for the SPAC merger and IPO. The company noted later in its report that expenses for raw materials last year would have been even higher, but the company had built up an unusually large inventory in 2023 which actually benefited the numbers for 2024.

In addition to the troubling trend with losses and higher expenses, the company is going through a serious cash drain that can be seen in more detail in the snapshot below. The company started 2023 with $20.7 million in cash on hand. By the end of that year, the number had been cut by over 50%, dropping to $9.3 million. As of December 31, 2024, FST reported having $5.1 million in cash on hand and other cash equivalents. While the rate of cash burn is slowing, it would appear that the company would need to find a way to raise funds before the end of Q4 2025. FST did note in its 2024 annual report that it will require "additional liquidity to continue as a going concern" within the next 12 months.

A deeper look at the 2024 balance sheet shows other issues for concern that are related to the company's cash problem. The company has a current ratio of 1.21 given the assets and liabilities stated below. That, by itself, is not overly concerning. However, FST's quick ratio of 0.55 shows that the company may have issues covering short-term liabilities, including the $15.3 million in short-term bank loans on the balance sheet. FST is already highly leveraged, as can be seen with its debt to equity ratio of 1.55. Raising additional fund through borrowing would likely be done with above market interest rates. Raising funds through authorization of additional common shares will dilute current shareholder's ownership of the company.

So is there any silver lining? Honestly, it is hard to find. The company is making efforts to lower costs. In the last couple of years, FST has diversified its product lines by offering graphite shafts. However, the company has outsourced that production. This has created added costs and complication to the production of these items. The company has plans to acquire its own graphite production operations in the near future.

The company will also benefit from the removal of the costs associated with the SPAC transition in the coming year, but even that is not enough to get the company back to profitability. The best chance for future success may depend on the success of the professional golfers that use FST shafts on the professional circuit, as explained below.

Industry Analysis and the Importance of Winning

At the end of 2024, the equipment manufacturing market for golf was projected to be about $10.5 billion. By 2033, that number is expected to be anywhere from $15.2 billion to $17.2 billion, with a compound average growth rate of 4.5% to 4.7%. The potential for profits within that space are variable, dependent on production costs and constantly changing prices for raw materials. Key drivers for growth include rising participation numbers from younger demographics, technological advancements for equipment, and greater popularity in Asia and the Middle East. Other tailwinds include a rising number of golf courses all over the world, an increase in golf tourism, and rising participation rates from female golfers.

Millennials are the biggest spenders on golf, with one estimate for annual golf expenditures for a year equaling just over $4,500. Golf has always been attractive to sponsors because the relatively high income of its participants when compared to most other sports. According to one survey published in Axios Atlanta, 43% of golfers plan to spend between $1000 and $2000 on golf clubs and accessories this year.

It is clear that the market for golf equipment is a large one, but it is also quite competitive. There are a number of players in this space that are much larger and much more established than FST, including TopGolf Callaway Brands, Taylor Made, PING, and Acushnet Holdings. TopGolf Callaway (MODG) and Acushnet (GOLF) are the only publicly traded companies in that group. GOLF is the only one that is profitable, as MODG has been struggling with its own issues with its sports entertainment sites. FST has significantly greater revenue growth than MODG or GOLF, but the company trails the others in almost all other meaningful financial metrics. GOLF does trade an earnings multiple of 18.6, so there is hope for share appreciation for FST if it can turn profitable, but if the stock were to trade at the same valuation, it would take $0.10 of earnings just to justify its current price.

Brand awareness is essential for FST, and the success of a few players can make a significant impact on company sales, as the company discovered last year. It should be no surprise that the sales of FST's products should be positively correlated with the success of the golfers who use them. The company confirmed this through a press release on May 8. Included in this update were updates from both the PGA Tour and LIV Tour.

From the PGA, golfers using FST equipment won six tournaments in 2024. In the 14 days following the end of each of these six tournaments, FST achieved sales increases that ranged from 9% to 102% when compared to the same period in 2023. The cumulative total increase in sales during these time periods was 40.3%. For the LIV Tour, the impact was similar, with two wins creating a cumulative increase in sales of its golf shafts of 47%. The impact of winning with LPGA events did not appear to have a significant impact on sales.

The current list of PGA professionals who are ranked in the Top 50 in the Official World Golf Ranking who are also using KBS equipment includes Akshay Bhatia, Cameron Davis, Justin Rose, Ludvig Aberg, Max Homa, Nicolai Hojgaard, Shane Lowry, Siwoo Kim, and Viktor Hovland. The company also has four strong LIV players - Dustin Johnson, Phil Mickelson, Jon Rahm, and Cameron Smith. LIV Tour players are not eligible for World Golf Rankings. The company has one win so far this year from Brian Campbell who won the 2025 Mexico Open in February.

Of course, greater sales mean little when your expenses are growing even faster. Over the last couple of years, FST's top-line growth has not been able to find its way to the bottom. Barring a long streak of wins by some of the golfers named above, it is hard to see a catalyst in the short term that is going to make a significant difference in the company's bottom line.

Risks to Consider

The company is not currently profitable, and there are no guarantees that this will change in the next 12 to 18 months. The company has been burning cash at an alarming rate over the last two years. FST has acknowledged the need for additional liquidity before the end of this year, and there are no good options for the company to raise cash at this point.

Geopolitical tension is a significant risk for FST in light of the current uncertainty around tariffs for Asian countries. Suppliers for the company are primarily based in China, Vietnam, and Taiwan. Added costs for imported materials would increase expenses for the company and decrease the chances for future profits. Tariffs could make FST's products more expensive in the US and negatively impact sales. While golf's popularity appears to be growing now, there are a number of economic and social factors that could inhibit that growth in the future. If fewer people are playing golf, demand for golf equipment would likely fall, even if equipment prices remain constant.

Although the company's stock is traded on NASDAQ, it is headquartered in Taiwan and provides very limited financial information on its website. Investors do not have access to detailed presentations and slide shows that most other companies produce for every quarter. Due diligence is strongly recommended for any business that has such a short history as a publicly traded company.

Conclusion

Buying shares in FST almost feels like placing a sports bet on a golfer with incredibly long odds. I do believe that some of the pessimism around the stock now is related to its unusual beginnings. The company's stock is now priced as if it may go out of business in the next six to nine months. If the company is going to turn things around, the company must find a way to control expenses and increase revenue at a rapid pace. The possibility of a share offering to attempt to raise additional cash is high. I am far too conservative to ever consider investing in a company like this. At the current time, I believe this stock is only worth holding for the most risk-tolerant investors.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters: Downgrade To Sell On Poor Demand And Execution
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO), which I downgraded to a hold rating as I believed FY25 was not going to be as good a performing year as I initially thought, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am downgrading my rating to a sell. My view is that the problems are now both external and internal, and the pathway to a fundamental recovery is a lot more uncertain than I had initially expected.

Investment Thesis

As a quick recap for the recent earnings, I'd like to highlight that AEO's latest 1Q25 results have validated my concerns that the demand environment has meaningfully deteriorated. Total net revenue declined 5% y/y, with comparable sales down 3%. The more concerning thing is how badly profitability collapsed. Gross margin fell an astonishing 1100 bps y/y to just 29.6%. While some pressure was expected due to a cautious consumer backdrop, a part of the decline was due to heavy markdowns and a major inventory write-down on underperforming spring and summer merchandise. This isn't just about weak traffic; it actually reflects the poor execution ability of AEO's management when times get tough. In particular, I think it signals that AEO product and pricing strategies are clearly misaligned with consumer demand.

The merchandising "miss" was particularly evident at Aerie, where key fashion items like tops and shorts failed to pick up consumer interest. To make things worse, these products carried higher input costs, which resulted in a sharp markdown-driven margin headwind. Meanwhile, the American Eagle brand did not have enough stock of its denim products (note that this is a core product of the brand), and it also saw weak demand for seasonal items like shorts and pants. From a broad-level perspective, this dual failure highlights major problems in inventory planning and merchandising strategy, which, I think, is fatal because the current uncertain demand backdrop is when management should be executing flawlessly.

In my view, these internal lapses make the recovery path far more uncertain. It's no longer just about waiting for the demand to rebound. AEO must fix its own house first, and that could take several quarters, if not longer.

The next bearish point is that the Aerie brand is no longer a growth driver for AEO. This brand used to be a steady grower, printing 8% and 5% comparable sales in FY23/24, but 1Q25 saw a 4% comparable sales decline (its first negative comp in years). While management pointed to continued growth in the OFFLINE activewear sub-brand, it doesn't matter because it is not enough to offset the weakness from the core Aerie brand (management has acknowledged this). If the weakness was due to one particular product "miss," it would not have been so bad. But it was broad-based and included core apparel categories like tops, shorts, and intimates (these are literally the foundational products of the Aerie brand).

The other key bearish factor is that I don't see any hope for a near-term rebound. Management's 2Q25 guidance basically cast this into stone, as they called for revenue to decline another 5% y/y, with comparable sales down 3%. This is essentially a repeat of 1Q25's poor performance. This signals that the demand and execution issues still persist. And the bigger problem is that earnings will likely stay depressed. The 2Q25 guidance calls for EBIT of just $40-$45 million, a substantial gap, down from $101 million in 2Q24. My view here is that AEO is forced to continue running elevated promotions, which, combined with weak full-price sell-through, leads to heavy margin pressure. The worst part of all this is that AEO is still working through excess inventory, which makes any near-term gross margin recovery even less plausible.

Valuation

Finally, the last nail in the coffin (in my decision to downgrade to a sell) was management's decision to withdraw FY25 guidance (basically proving my view right that AEO was going to miss FY25 estimates). The reason I highlight this is that this came at precisely the time when investors needed clarity the most. With no forward guidance, this likely forces consensus and the market to take a very risk-off view and be conservative in their estimates (you can see consensus revising their estimates down immediately after the earnings). This also casts further doubts on the FY26 EBIT margin target of 10% and revenue CAGR of 3 to 5%.

For a stock that is already under pressure, this elevated uncertainty simply increases the range of outcomes (i.e., more downside risk). In my view, the worst may not be over. All the data points and management comments point to continued weakness ahead, and while consensus has brought down their estimates, the general market still seems positive on a recovery as the stock valuation (forward EBITDA) has re-rated upwards to 3.9x (from 2.9x previously). I don't see a strong justification for this upgrade, and if anything, I think this simply increased the room for downside when AEO prints another quarter of poor earnings. For comparison, Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) trades just at 3.6x forward EBITDA but has positive growth expectations vs. AEO with a negative growth outlook.

Risk

If macro conditions improve and discretionary spending rebounds, AEO could benefit meaningfully, which would not only drive earnings growth but also trigger the consensus to upgrade its estimates. Also, if AEO manages to clear inventory faster than expected, it could have a clear inventory position heading into back-to-school, which opens up the possibility for better margin and sales upside.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for AEO is a sell. The demand backdrop remains weak, but more importantly, AEO's internal execution has not been great, and that significantly raises the risk profile. With Aerie now printing negative comparable sales, core merchandising issues unresolved, and FY25 guidance withdrawn, I struggle to find a near-term catalyst that would justify the recent multiple re-rating.

data.title: A Deep Dive On Nebius
data.description:
Introduction

The broader indices are currently trading near all-time highs, with many stocks, including those that I hold, up more than 100% over the last few years.

As a growth investor, I'm always on the lookout for the next multi-bagger stock, but boy, is it difficult to find high-quality, undervalued stocks in this kind of market.

On a side note, my investing style revolves around finding companies that are growing rapidly, are improving profitability, have little to no debt, are founder-led, and offer significant upside potential.

After searching for months, one particular name piqued my interest: Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS). Not because NBIS is one of the most trending stocks on X, but because of its origins, its story, and its valuation.

It's probably been two months since I began researching the company, so today, I want to share with you my initial thoughts and analysis on NBIS. Hopefully, you'll find value in it.

Without further ado, let's jump in.

Company: Building AI Factories

I want to start by saying that I'm no engineer or tech expert, so my take on the technical aspect of NBIS's business is rather limited. As an average investor with average tech knowledge, reading through NBIS's annual report and website gave me headaches, as there were so many technical terms and jargon that were far beyond my comprehension. Perhaps, this is one shortcoming of my investment thesis on NBIS—that I lack understanding of the company's technology.

Slurm, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL—I have no idea what these are. But that's okay. I don't have to know all the nitty-gritty details of the business. As long as I understand the big picture—what the company does on a high level, where the company is heading, and how its financials look—then that's good enough.

Having said that, if I had to sum up what NBIS does in one super short sentence, it would be this: NBIS builds AI factories.

Per NVIDIA (NVDA), here's the definition of an AI factory:

An AI factory is a specialized computing infrastructure designed to create value from data by managing the entire AI life cycle, from data ingestion to training, fine-tuning, and high-volume AI inference. The primary product is intelligence, measured by token throughput, which drives decisions, automation, and new AI solutions.

(NVIDIA: What is an AI Factory?)

In essence, NBIS's AI factories consist of cost-efficient data centers, high-performance GPU clusters, in-house hardware, a proprietary cloud platform, and deep AI expertise, capable of supporting the entire AI life cycle—all in one place.

It is the company's full-stack approach that sets it apart from competitors. Compared to bare-metal providers and GPU clouds, NBIS offers more flexibility and additional tools for AI workloads. NBIS also provides highly customizable solutions as compared to hyperscalers like AWS (AMZN), Google Cloud (GOOG), and Microsoft Azure (MSFT), where they often take a one-size-fits-all approach that may not cater to the demands of AI-specific workloads.

Put simply, NBIS has full control over the whole AI infrastructure value chain, including hardware and software, and that allows developers and enterprises to receive all their AI needs in a single platform, instead of dealing with multiple vendors.

Being vertically integrated also means that NBIS has a cost advantage. Thanks to its in-house hardware design (servers, racks, and data centers), NBIS has a 20% to 25% lower total cost of GPU ownership and operations compared to competitors.

Subsequently, this allows NBIS to pass on the savings to clients in the form of lower prices, which, on top of its best-in-class AI infrastructure, makes its value proposition all the more attractive.

According to a SemiAnalysis report, NBIS's ODM strategy dramatically reduces initial hardware costs and ongoing operating expenses, allowing the company to offer "the lowest absolute price and the best terms for short to medium-term rents."

Nebius currently stands out by offering the best absolute pricing while still maintaining robust technical capabilities. Their approach involves aggressive cost optimization strategies, including the adoption of Original Design Manufacturer (ODM) hardware for GPU servers, significantly lowering their total cost of ownership. For example, by bypassing traditional OEM providers like Dell or Supermicro, which can price servers with as high as 10-15% gross margins, Nebius achieves cost reductions through a custom-designed ODM chassis, minimizing gross margins from a typical 10-15% down to around 2%.

(SemiAnalysis: The GPU Cloud ClusterMAX™ Rating System)

Based on a set of evaluation criteria—including security, price for value, technical expertise, and reliability—the same SemiAnalysis report also placed NBIS in the Gold tier, which puts the company in the same league as Oracle (ORCL) and Microsoft Azure, while being ahead of AWS and Google Cloud. This speaks volumes about NBIS's AI infrastructure. However, it falls behind CoreWeave (CRWV).

Not only that, but NBIS also offers one of, if not the most cost-efficient AI software in the market. For instance, NBIS recently launched AI Studio, an inference-as-a-service platform that gives AI app developers access to some of the most prominent open-source AI models, including DeepSeek and Meta Llama (META).

An independent study by Artificial Analysis found that NBIS AI Studio offers one of the most affordable endpoints for accessing a frontier model, with prices that are nearly half of what other big-name providers offer, while, at the same time, producing top-tier performance. As you can see, when using the Meta Llama and Alibaba Qwen (BABA) models, NBIS AI Studio lands in the most attractive quadrant of the Outspeed vs. Price chart.

Because of its excellent value and pricing flexibility, NBIS's offerings are ideal for independent developers and startups, which happen to be the bulk of their customer base. If you check NBIS's customer success stories, you'll probably have never heard of the companies mentioned there, as they are still in their infancy.

Having a startup-heavy client base could be a double-edged sword for NBIS. On one hand, churn could be high, as these companies are likely still unprofitable and financially unstable. On the other hand, NBIS could benefit immensely from their growth as they are likely to increase platform usage.

Nevertheless, as NBIS expands its data center footprint and compute capacity, management intends to target frontier AI labs and enterprise customers, both of which require cost-effective, computationally intensive workloads.

It's also worth mentioning that the NBIS cloud platform leverages NVIDIA's GPUs, enabling clients to scale AI workloads at max performance. Through its long-standing partnership with NVIDIA, NBIS has preferential access to NVIDIA chips, which are currently facing supply constraints, so access to these chips, including the latest and greatest ones, allows NBIS to install these chips in their factories as soon as possible, and thus, positions NBIS well to acquire clients with serious AI ambitions.

Case in point. In Q1 alone, NBIS made several announcements in regard to its partnership with NVIDIA:

  • NBIS will be one of the first AI clouds to offer the new NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra AI Factory Platform.
  • NBIS became a launch partner for NVIDIA Dynamo.
  • NBIS named as one of only six companies to be a Reference Platform NVIDIA Cloud Partner.
  • NBIS will support the NVIDIA DGX Cloud Lepton marketplace at launch.

I won't get into the technical details of these partnerships, but the fact of the matter is that being a partner to the world's most valuable company and arguably the most important company in the AI age gives NBIS immense technological credibility and brand reputation among developers and enterprises.

Mind you, NVIDIA does not give away its chips to just any company, so partnering with NBIS to deliver its chips to the market is in itself a confirmation of NBIS's high-quality AI factory offering. Not to mention, NVIDIA is also an investor in NBIS, a clear sign that it is committed to NBIS's long-term growth.

Speaking of which, NBIS has a unique funding strength with the ability to tap capital markets and its stakes in non-core businesses to raise additional capital to scale its AI factories. As you know, the data center business is highly capital-intensive, so access to capital is crucial for long-term success.

Perhaps, investors are confident that the NBIS team will generate significant shareholder value. After all, the NBIS team has an excellent track record of building and scaling companies, including Yandex, the so-called "Google of Russia".

With all this in mind, I think NBIS's competitive advantage comes down to a few key factors:

  • NBIS provides a full-stack AI infrastructure, with complete control of the entire AI value chain.
  • NBIS offers the best price-to-value AI infrastructure offering, both on the hardware and software side.

  • NBIS enjoys high barriers to entry, as the AI factory business requires access to the best engineers, large amounts of capital, and special equipment like GPUs.

In addition to its core AI factory business, NBIS also operates several other businesses—namely Toloka, TripleTen, and Avride—but I won't get into them (at least for now) as I think they'll eventually be divested for the purpose of raising funds to scale its AI factory unit. I'll talk more about the non-core businesses in the valuation section of this article.

In short, NBIS is building AI factories that power the AI revolution. It offers one of, if not the most compelling AI infrastructure-as-a-service offering, backed by an all-in-one AI platform, industry-low prices, and deep AI expertise. Considering all this, I believe NBIS will be a serious contender in the growing AI space for many years to come.

Growth: All Systems Go

Aside from my lack of technical understanding, another limitation to analyzing NBIS is the lack of historical financial data. For now, we have only eight quarters of data, and what's more, NBIS is growing from a relatively tiny base, so one, growth rates look unsustainably high at the moment, and two, it's difficult to estimate NBIS's baseline growth rate.

That said, let's take a look at NBIS's financials, starting with growth. Keep in mind that I'll be focusing my analysis on NBIS's core infrastructure business, as the other businesses are likely to be divested, and thus, be deconsolidated from NBIS Group's financials.

Group Revenue came in at $55M in Q1, up 385% YoY, with the core business accounting for nearly 75% of the Group's Revenue. As you can see below, NBIS is growing at a rapid clip, particularly due to its small scale.

I do want to point out the sequential Revenue drop from Q3 to Q4, as it was one of the few negative things that caught my attention during my research. As it turned out, the Revenue decline in Q4 was primarily due to several reasons, namely:

  1. Longer customer acquisition lead times due to customers being more selective and wanting to do extensive testing.
  2. Loss of a few large customers in Q4. This was anticipated, but the longer lead times prevented NBIS from replacing them before the end of the year.
  3. Customer migration to a new AI cloud platform, which took some resources and led to some downtime.

Fortunately, "these were mostly timing issues" that should not be repeated in future quarters.

Nevertheless, I think it's important to bring this up as it puts into context the stability of NBIS's Revenue coming from its core AI factory business—losing a couple of large customers doesn't really translate to a sticky business model, so keep this in mind.

On the bright side, demand rebounded sharply in Q1, with Annualized Run-rate Revenue [ARR] for the core business up 684% YoY and 175% QoQ, to $249M. This was mainly driven by increased GPU capacity and strong sales execution, resulting in a record number of new customers.

Management also mentioned that they sold out most of their capacity by the end of Q1, so that could be a bottleneck for growth. But rest assured, the company continues to see momentum in Q2, with April ARR of $310M, which is a significant step up from March levels. They're also seeing this momentum going into May.

By the end of 2025, management expects an ARR of up to $1B, which is more than a 10x increase from its ARR of $90M at the end of 2024.

Yes. Blistering hot growth.

At the same time, management expects full-year 2025 Revenue of $500M to $700M, which is about a 4x YoY increase at the midpoint of the guidance. Keep in mind that this figure does not yet reflect the deconsolidation of Toloka, the group's AI data labeling business, which recently received an investment led by Bezos Expeditions. For reference, Toloka is expected to generate $50M to $70M of Revenue in 2025.

In the medium term, management is targeting mid-single-digit billion dollars in Revenue. According to them, "medium term" means "a few years", which is quite vague in my opinion.

To achieve this growth, NBIS has to scale its AI factory footprint. Its Finland data center alone could scale to 75MW by the end of the year, with the potential to generate $1B of ARR at full capacity. Its New Jersey data center has a capacity of up to 300MW, with the first phase expected to be available as early as summer 2025. Adding its other factories across the globe, NBIS has secured expansion capacity to over 400MW.

Per management, they expect 100MW of capacity by the end of 2025. Considering their ARR guidance of about $1B, simple math gets us to $10M of ARR per MW capacity.

Assuming its full capacity of 400MW, NBIS's current AI factory footprint has the potential to generate $4B of ARR.

I don't know when NBIS will get to this run rate, but know that NBIS is ramping capacity rapidly, so this could very well materialize within "a few years".

For instance, NBIS began 2025 with only about 25MW of capacity, so they will be increasing capacity by 300% this year, to 100MW. Assuming capacity growth of about 100%, 50%, and 30% in 2026, 2027, and 2028, respectively, NBIS could reach that 400MW mark by the end of 2028—thus, achieving the $4B ARR.

This falls neatly within management's medium-term target.

Also note that my oversimplified projection does not take into consideration new data center expansion—it only accounts for NBIS's existing portfolio of AI factories, so my projections could be conservative.

Having said that, I think 400MW of capacity and $4B of ARR within three years is perfectly doable. Analysts, for one, expect Revenue—not ARR—of $3.9B by 2028, which should translate to an ARR figure of higher than $4B.

Also worth pointing out is that analysts expect only $526M of Revenue in 2025, which is near the low end of management's guidance of $500M to $700M. Given its current momentum, I think NBIS will beat this low bar of an estimate easily.

Regardless, NBIS's ultimate growth story revolves around capitalizing on the AI revolution. With that, management plans to build their data center pipeline to provide scalability to more than 1GW of capacity.

They realize the speed at which the AI market is growing, which is why they are scaling the company as aggressively as possible—through significant data center and GPU capacity expansion—hoping to capture a decent chunk of the $260B+ GPU-as-a-service and AI cloud market.

Judging by recent business momentum and developments, it looks like all systems go for NBIS.

Profitability: Scale is Necessary

NBIS's Cost of Revenue mainly consists of the costs of owning and renting data centers, so as NBIS scales its data center footprint, Cost of Revenue will naturally increase.

In Q1, Cost of Revenue was $30M, up 231% YoY. Though this growth rate looks concerning, it is 154pp lower than Revenue growth. As a result, Cost of Revenue as a % of Revenue dropped 25pp YoY, to 53%. This translates to a Gross Profit of $26M at a 47% Margin.

As NBIS scales further, NBIS should gain economies of scale, leading to higher Gross Margins and earnings potential. For reference, CoreWeave, which has a similar business model as NBIS and a much larger business from a Revenue perspective, boasts a Gross Margin profile of more than 70%, so we can expect NBIS's Gross Margin to improve further, to at least 70%.

Operating Profit is still negative at $(130)M, representing a (234)% margin. This is unsurprising since all of NBIS's businesses are still in their early stages, with ongoing investments to grow its team, drive customer adoption, and support product innovation.

For example, NBIS has been building its sales and go-to-market team over the last few quarters, which is paying off handsomely—the team contributed to record new customers and a near-max sell-out of its capacity in Q1.

Additionally, NBIS is investing heavily in data center hardware and GPUs, which subsequently drives higher Depreciation and Amortization. In Q1, D&A was $49M, or 89% of Revenue, which is an increase from 78% last year.

Keep in mind that depreciation begins as soon as NBIS takes delivery of these fixed assets, while Revenue generation comes with a lag as NBIS has to take some time to onboard clients. So, in the short term, D&A growth may outpace Revenue growth. Nevertheless, given the CapEx-heavy nature of the data center business, D&A will likely become the largest component of the company's Operating Expenses in the future.

We can see the same thing for its bottom-line metrics. Whether we look at it from a GAAP or Non-GAAP perspective, NBIS is still very much unprofitable.

  • Net Income was $(114)M at a (205)% margin.
  • Adjusted Net Income was $(93)M at a (167)% margin.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $(63)M at a (113)% margin.

However, we can see that NBIS's profit margins are trending in the right direction, so as the company continues to grow its Revenue base, we can expect NBIS to gain operating leverage, thus inching closer towards profitability.

In fact, management expects NBIS to turn Adjusted EBITDA positive by the second half of 2025. I think this will be driven by two key factors:

  • Continued expansion of the core AI factory business.
  • Divestments of its non-core assets, which account for more than half of the Group's Adjusted EBITDA loss in 2024 and Q1. Toloka will be deconsolidated in Q2 onwards, which should boost Adjusted EBITDA by about $10M in Q2. For context, Toloka's Adjusted EBITDA in 2024 was $(40)M.

In the "medium term" (there goes that phrase again), management anticipates Adjusted EBIT Margins of 20% to 30%, with the potential to "go well north of 30% in the longer term".

What I like about this target is that it includes the hefty D&A line, so a 20% to 30% Adjusted EBIT Margin within the next few years is quite impressive.

Looking at the Operating Margins of some of the most prominent cloud infrastructure providers, it's safe to say that NBIS's margin target is well within reach—too conservative, perhaps.

  • Google Cloud Operating Margin = 16%
  • Microsoft Intelligent Cloud Operating Margin = 42%
  • AWS Operating Margin = 38%
  • Oracle Operating Margin = 32%
  • CoreWeave Adjusted Operating Margin = 18%

But first, NBIS needs to scale. It needs to add more data centers, acquire more GPUs, and run its AI factories at a high enough occupancy rate to cover its high fixed cost base.

Only then will NBIS turn profitable and cash-flow positive, and start to generate immense shareholder value for years to come.

Health: Good for the Rest of 2025

NBIS requires a significant amount of capital to scale. So far, the company has tapped a variety of funding sources to raise capital, including:

  • $2.8B from the sale of its businesses in Russia and certain international markets (July 2024).
  • $0.7B strategic equity raise from a group of investors, including Accel, NVIDIA, and Orbis (December 2024).
  • $1.0B private placement of senior unsecured convertible notes (June 2025).

Despite these capital raises, NBIS's cash level is depleting rapidly, reflecting the company's aggressive CapEx cadence.

As of Q1, NBIS has $1.4B of Cash with virtually zero debt. As you can see below, Cash levels dropped by about $1.0B QoQ, mainly due to an Adjusted EBITDA loss of $63M and CapEx of $544M in Q1.

Since management guided for $2B of CapEx in 2025, that leaves about $1.5B of CapEx allocated for the rest of the year.

With the recent $1.0B notes offering, NBIS has about $2.4B of Cash in hand, which is sufficient to cover the company's CapEx target alongside its day-to-day expenses.

In other words, NBIS is in a solid financial standing, at least for the remainder of 2025.

One thing worth pointing out is that the initial conversion price of the convertible notes was a 40% premium ($51 per share) over the share price when the notes offering was announced ($37 per share). From the investor's perspective, a premium is always good as it minimizes shareholder dilution and signals strong institutional confidence in future valuation growth.

That said, NBIS has substantial firepower to invest in growth. Even so, NBIS can still raise capital without diluting shareholders, that is, through divesting its non-core businesses.

As it stands, NBIS has stakes/ownerships in four other businesses:

  • 100% ownership in Avride, a developer of autonomous technologies.
  • 100% ownership in TripleTen, an edtech platform.
  • a majority stake in Toloka, an AI data labeling company.
  • a 28% stake in ClickHouse, an online analytical processing database.

‍Should NBIS need additional capital, NBIS could liquidate any of these companies—management has made it clear that they intend to do this to fund capacity expansion for its core AI factory business.

Otherwise, NBIS could hold on to these stakes and benefit from all the growth and upside.

Okay, that seems like a good segue into the next topic.

Valuation: 300%+ Upside Potential

Let me attempt to value NBIS using sum-of-the-parts analysis, starting with the non-core businesses.

  • Toloka – management expects 2025 Toloka Revenue of $50M to $70M, so $60M at the midpoint, which is a 127% YoY growth over 2024. Applying a very conservative 10x multiple, Toloka would be worth around $600M today (with that kind of growth rate in an AI-hyped market, a 20x multiple is justifiable... but I want to be conservative). Despite recently giving up majority voting control, NBIS retains a significant majority economic stake. Assuming NBIS still has a 60% stake, its Toloka equity stake would be worth about $360M today.
  • TripleTen – management expects 2025 TripleTen Revenue of $40M to $60M, so $50M at the midpoint, which is a 74% YoY growth over 2024. Udemy (UDMY) and Coursera (COUR) have a multiple of 2x and 3x, respectively, with 0% and 5% growth, respectively. Given TripleTen's superior growth rate, I'll slap in a 5x multiple, translating to a $250M stake for NBIS.
  • Avride – this one is a little tricky, but here goes nothing. Looking at one of the slides in NBIS's 2024 Investor Presentation, it looks like Avride (22M+ km) has accumulated more autonomous miles than players like GM-owned Cruise (8M+ km) and Amazon-owned Zoox (1.6M+ km). In 2021, Cruise was valued at approximately $30B but has since been cut by more than half, so about $15B give or take. In 2018, Zoox was valued at $3.2B. If we look at mileage alone, Avride should be valued more than Cruise and Zoox. However, we need to factor in Cruise's and Zoox's access to their parent company's network and resources, so they deserve a little bit of a premium. Interestingly, back in 2021, Morgan Stanley valued "Yandex's Driverless Company" at $7B, but given that 2021 was a year of peak euphoria and absurd valuations, I'm going to assume a mere $1B valuation for Avride today, which is quite pessimistic considering that it logged more miles than Cruise and Zoox. In 2022, many growth stocks crashed by more than 50% to 90%, which is why I have decided to cut Avride's valuation by that much.

  • ClickHouse – this one's easy. ClickHouse was last valued at $6.35B, which means NBIS's 28% equity stake is worth about $1.8B.

Adding them all together, NBIS's non-core assets are worth about $3.4B—and these are based on conservative assumptions.

As of this writing, NBIS trades at a Market Cap of $11.5B. Subtracting its non-core assets, NBIS's core AI factory business is probably worth about $8B.

Let's now evaluate how much its core business could be worth in a few years.

Looking at NBIS's historical valuation multiple seems redundant, given that the company is still building out its data centers and has yet to utilize them fully.

If you barely know a thing or two about NBIS and just look at NBIS's current Price to Sales multiple, you'll quickly realize that NBIS is one heck of an expensive stock with a multiple of 72x.

Fortunately, you've read my article up to this point, so you know a thing or two about NBIS. More specifically, you know that NBIS is a company in hypergrowth mode. As such, it's more appropriate to look at the company's forward multiples.

I've shown you this table in an earlier section before, but I want you to focus on the "FWD Price/Sales" column this time round. As you can see, NBIS's Price to Sales Ratio is expected to collapse rapidly, driven by an explosion in Revenue over the next few years.

  • Current Price to Sales of 72x, based on $161M of TTM Revenue.
  • Price to 2025 Expected Sales of 29x, based on $526M of Revenue.
  • Price to 2028 Expected Sales of 4x, based on $3.9B of Revenue.

Now, a Price to Sales Ratio of 4x in 2028 doesn't sound too expensive. In fact, it might be cheap considering its expected growth rate.

If we look at fellow infrastructure-as-a-service providers Oracle and CoreWeave, we can see that they have a Forward Price to Sales Ratio of 8x and 16x, respectively. Let's use Oracle's multiple since it's a more mature business.

Assuming NBIS achieves $3.9B of Revenue by 2028, I believe NBIS's valuation should at least match Oracle's multiple of 8x, instead of the 4x multiple shown in the table above. This should translate to a Market Cap of about $32B by 2028, just for the core AI factory business alone.

In other words, there's a good chance that NBIS's core business expands from $8B today to $32B by 2028—a 300% increase from here.

Bear in mind that I'm assuming this $3.9B Revenue estimate will come entirely from its core business, so zero Revenue from TripleTen and Avride, since I think they will eventually be divested. In addition, the $32B Market Cap estimate does NOT include cash and the equity value of its non-core businesses, which could be higher in the next few years, thus being value accretive to NBIS's Market Cap.

Granted, my valuation analysis may be far from accurate. For one, I'm using Price to Sales multiples—not the best valuation metric to use. Furthermore, I'm using Revenue estimates coming from just two analysts, so there's a wide margin of error here. And also, I have not considered NBIS's margin profile in my analysis. Therefore, I'd have to urge readers to take my valuation approach with a grain of salt.

I must admit, figuring out the value of NBIS today is a real challenge. What I'm confident in is that NBIS will be a much larger company within the next few years—and that's all that matters.

It's also worth noting that NBIS has a high insider ownership of about 17.3%, with the Founder and CEO, Arkady Volozh, owning 12.9% of the whole company. Nice to see management with skin in the game and strong alignment of interests with shareholders.

Arkady Volozh built Yandex into a $30B company.

Perhaps, he can do the same for NBIS.

Risks

Here are a few risks to consider:

  • Competition: The cloud infrastructure-as-a-service market is highly competitive with big-time players like Amazon, Oracle, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave. While NBIS has an edge over them due to its full-stack, AI-focused infrastructure, the hyperscalers may launch an AI cloud computing service as well, thus competing directly with NBIS. This could pressure NBIS's growth. In addition, increased competition could lead to the commoditization of these services, leading to lower prices and lower profit margins.
  • Capital Constraints: NBIS's growth depends on significant capital infusion. If, let's say, the capital markets go haywire due to a recession, a tariff war, or whatnot, capital investors may turn cautious, thus deploying less of their capital. Furthermore, a stock market downturn may lower the implied valuations of NBIS's non-core assets, thus providing NBIS with fewer funds if management chooses to divest them. That said, failure to secure sufficient capital would hinder NBIS's growth.
  • NVIDIA Reliance: As discussed earlier, NBIS relies on NVIDIA chips to power its AI factories. Without NVIDIA, NBIS might not even exist. If there's any supply chain disruption, if there's a significant price hike on the chips, or if NBIS loses preferential access to the chips, NBIS's operations would take a turn for the worse.
  • CoreWeave Bubble Pop: As you know, CoreWeave just went public in March this year, and in just three months, the stock is already up 300%+. NBIS stock became a big beneficiary of the CoreWeave IPO hype, rallying more than 100% in the same timeframe. Now, I don't know much about CoreWeave, its fundamentals, and its valuation, but I know parabolic moves like this are rarely sustainable, so at some point, we may see the bubble burst, thus dragging NBIS down as well.

Thesis

AI is taking the world by storm, and developers, startups, and enterprises all across the globe are scrambling to capitalize on the AI revolution.

The problem? Lack of infrastructure to support the rapid growth of the AI industry.

Enter NBIS.

In a nutshell, NBIS is building AI factories to service the emerging AI industry. Its competitive advantages include its vertically integrated AI infrastructure, industry-leading price-to-value, and unique access to scarce resources, all of which should support robust growth for many years to come.

While NBIS is still unprofitable, the company is scaling aggressively, and over time, economies of scale should allow it to turn a profit, which will be a major catalyst for the stock.

As it stands, NBIS is still in its early stages of growth, only beginning to grow its global AI infrastructure footprint. In the long term, management envisions achieving more than 1GW of GPU capacity—a 40x increase from the start of 2025.

Today, investors get to piggyback a pure-play AI giant in the making. Valuation may look expensive today, but if NBIS can reach 1GW of capacity in the next "few years", then the stock looks attractive at these levels. Also, don't forget its non-core assets, which could be worth billions of dollars.

That being said, I am initiating coverage on NBIS with a Buy rating.

As for me, I need to do a little bit more digging on the company—I'll stay on the sidelines for now.

data.title: June FOMC Meeting: Ignore The Dot Plot In This Uncertain Environment
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

At its June meeting, the Federal Open Market Committee decided to keep its benchmark policy rate unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% as expected.

The new Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) showed a weaker economy in 2025, a marginal increase in the unemployment rate, and a higher inflation forecast, while the median dot plot remained unchanged at two rate cuts for this year.

Yet, with Fed Chair Powell emphasizing the elevated state of uncertainty around their policy outlook (“With uncertainty as elevated as it is, no one holds these rate paths with a lot of conviction”), he essentially de-emphasized the dot plot.

The key takeaway from the June meeting came not from the SEP, but from Powell’s remark during the press conference: “For the time being, we are well-positioned to wait to learn more about the likely course of the economy before considering any adjustments to our policy stance.” In essence, the continued strength of the U.S. economy gives the Fed room to pause and wait for clearer data before making any policy changes.

Current assessment

The U.S. economy has defied recession fears, with hard data cooling only slightly in recent months. The labor market is showing only modest signs of cracks, while inflation has surprised on the downside of late.

Recent surveys suggest that firms are likely to pass through some or all of their tariff-induced costs, so an inflation boost is increasingly likely in Q3. Tariff uncertainty has likely peaked, but it has been replaced by growing fiscal and geopolitical uncertainty – creating a difficult environment for forecasters.

From Fed Chair Powell’s perspective, the continued robustness of the economy suggests that the current policy stance is only modestly restrictive, in turn indicating only a shallow cutting cycle is likely over the forecast horizon.

He noted that while inflation is likely to move higher over the coming months as tariffs are passed along the supply chain, it is highly unclear if the tariff-driven inflation will be short-lived or persistent.

Certainly, the risk that unanchored inflation expectations - even in the short term - leads to persistent inflation is heightened, especially in an environment where overall price stability has not yet been achieved. Against that backdrop, the Fed has little reason to cut rates preemptively.

Updates to the Summary of Economic Projections

The new Summary of Economic Projections was also released on Wednesday. In general, it showed the Fed moving further away from both parts of its dual mandate, underscoring the difficult dilemma the central bank currently faces.

  • The GDP growth forecast for 2025 was revised down from 1.7% to 1.4%, from 1.8% to 1.6% in 2026, and was left unchanged at 1.8% for 2027.
  • The unemployment rate forecast was revised up slightly, from 4.4% to 4.5% in 2025, and remaining there in 2026.
  • The headline PCE inflation forecast for 2025 was revised up, from 2.7% to 3%. With headline inflation currently sitting at just 2.1%, the Fed is projecting a sharp increase in inflation over the coming months. Importantly, though, inflation is then projected to fall back to 2.4% in 2026, suggesting that the Fed expects the tariff impact on inflation to be fairly short-lived (transitory!)

The revised SEP forecasts for 2025 are very similar to our own. We expect GDP growth this year of 1.5%, inflation to reach 3.2%, and unemployment to rise to 4.5%.

Despite the changes to the inflation forecasts, there was no change to the 2025 median dot plot, which still shows two cuts this year. The plot was finely split: 10 participants projected two or more cuts in 2025, and 9 participants projected one cut or no cuts.

Overall, there was a slight hawkish shift in the distribution of dots for this year, with 7 of 19 participants now seeing no cuts this year, up from 4 participants in March.

The median dot now sees just one cut in 2026, down from two cuts in the March SEP, and one more cut in 2027, ultimately taking the Fed funds rate down to 3.375%. The median longer-run dot was unchanged at 3%.

FOMC dot projections

June 2025

Source: Federal Reserve, Clearnomics, Principal Asset Management. Data as of June 18, 2025.

Policy outlook

The unchanged median dot showing 50 bps of cuts for this year despite the higher inflation outlook is somewhat surprising. Yet, any change in this year’s dot plot may have been interpreted as a signal that the Fed has a clear understanding about its future policy path.

As Powell clearly conveyed throughout the press conference, with the economic outlook still very much shrouded in uncertainty, the Fed is still unsure of how things will pan out.

With inflation already running above target and economic data still looking resilient, the current environment of persistent uncertainty is ripe ground for a policy misstep. The wisest decision for the Fed is to simply stand still – both on its current policy rates and its dot plot. Our view is that the Fed will remain on hold until late Q4, with just one 25 bp cut this year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oracle's Multi-Cloud Strategy Shift Is Proving To Be A Success
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL)(NEOE:ORAC:CA) is beginning to be recognized as a major cloud player amongst its larger hyperscaler peers. With RPO expected to double in eFY26, Oracle is accelerating the development of its data center capacity with a $25b capital outlay for the fiscal year. With enterprises increasing the adoption of GenAI and agentic AI, Oracle should realize substantial consumption growth across its cloud platform, driving top-line growth in eFY26.

Despite the substantial growth outlay, power capacity remains constrained and may push out or delay some large-scale projects.

Going into a position, I believe investors should be cautious about the premium, which currently sits at a relative high at 22.40x EV/aEBITDA on a trailing twelve-month basis. I believe investors should be patient and wait for weakness in the share price before initiating or building a position. I personally reduced my position following the q4’25 earnings release and will be looking for a new entry point. My suggested reentry point would be $191/share at the midpoint average of ORCL shares' historical trading premium at 20.54x EV/aEBITDA.

Placing a value on the firm, I believe ORCL shares should be priced at $271/share at 19.69x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending ORCL shares with a STRONG BUY.

Oracle Corporation Operations

Oracle reported $138b in remaining performance obligations in q4’25, a 41% year-over-year increase, driven by significant demand for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure [OCI]. As management reiterated in the q4’25 earnings call, “demand continues to dramatically outstrip supply.” Management is anticipating OCI to grow by over 70% in eFY26, a major acceleration from the 50% year-over-year growth rate realized in FY25 results. With demand for cloud services remaining strong, management is anticipating RPO to expand by 100% in eFY26.

One of the driving growth factors will be Oracle Autonomous Database. This is drawn upon the increase in data produced with the emergence of GenAI. For instance, when ChatGPT was released in 2022, data created reached 101 zettabytes, 50x the amount generated 12 years prior. It is estimated that 552 zettabytes will be generated between 2024-2026 as a result of the mass adoption of AI applications. I suspect that this figure may be light, given that only 78% of organizations have adopted some form of AI technologies. As agentic AI is further adopted, I believe data generation will substantially accelerate, driving the need for storage capacity. In q4’24, CyberArk (CYBR) reported that there were 82 machine identities for every one human in organizations worldwide. With the assumption that only 78% of organizations are currently leveraging AI, I suspect there will be significant scaling over the next year, potentially overshooting estimates by a wide margin.

Enterprise AI adoption is expected to significantly drive consumption for Oracle as customer-specific data is utilized in AI applications and large language models [LLMs]. Having the capability of developing and deploying AI agents in one ecosystem should drive significant growth.

Oracle also realized substantial growth from its MultiCloud database strategy, growing 115% sequentially in q4’25. This component of the business involves partnership across the major hyperscalers, including Amazon (AMZN) AWS, Alphabet (GOOG) Google Cloud, and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure. As part of this integrated partnership, Oracle has 23 data center locations; additionally, it has 47 in development over the next 12 months. Management anticipates momentum in the MultiCloud business to continue in eFY26, growing at a triple-digit percentage rate.

In addition to this, Oracle Cloud@Customer has grown substantially, with 29 dedicated data centers live and another 30 being built in eFY26. Oracle Cloud@Customer is essentially a managed private cloud data center completely outfitted with OCI. A few of the benefits of Cloud@Customer is that it provides organizations with the same autonomous capabilities in the database while providing security and governance.

This could potentially drive revenue in Oracle’s IaaS platform, which realized a 59% year-over-year increase in consumption revenue in FY25.

Oracle Corporation Financial Position

Looking to eq1’26, I’m forecasting Oracle to generate $15b in net revenue, with an adjusted EPS of $1.48/share. I believe growth will primarily be driven by strong cloud migrations in the coming fiscal year as more data center capacity comes online. Though management indicated that the Project Stargate hasn’t impacted RPO, I suspect there should be a ramp-up in the coming years as power capacity remains a headwind for the Texas location.

Oracle reported a -$394mm cash outflow for FY25, primarily the result of significant investments in data center equipment. Capital investments for FY25 increased by over 2x as a result of these large investments. Despite the cash outflow experienced in the fiscal year, these investments should cycle through to the top line in the coming fiscal year, driving significant revenue growth for the organization. Looking to eFY26, management guided a capital outlay of $25b+ to close the gap between capacity and demand. This could potentially place Oracle in a consecutive fiscal year of cash outflow.

Risks Related to Oracle Corporation

Bull Case

Oracle is building out significant data center capacity eFY26, providing a major ramp-up for cloud revenue growth. With enterprises turning to Oracle Cloud Database for autonomous database operations, I believe Oracle will realize substantial growth in consumption, particularly as more processes are automated with agentic AI.

Bear Case

ORCL shares are trading at an excessive premium to their hyperscaler peers, potentially adding significant near-term risk to gyration or revaluation. Power capacity continues to be a constraint for large data center build-outs, which can be confirmed by organizations like GE Vernova (GEV) and Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMNEY), both of which have gas turbine manufacturing backlogs extending beyond 2028. This could potentially delay large build-outs and push out revenue growth for Oracle.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

On an unadjusted basis, ORCL shares have turned to trading at a significant premium above its peer hyperscalers at 27.76x EV/EBITDA.

Despite the high premium, I believe the valuation is justified given Oracle’s significant growth outlay across enterprise customers. With more workloads migrating to the cloud to enable autonomous database operations, I believe Oracle’s growth may continue to remain strong for the coming years.

Given the high premium and recent run-up in valuation, I suspect shares may experience some gyration in the near-term, potentially opening up a better opportunity for building one’s position. I recently reduced my ORCL position with this in mind. My suggested reentry point would be $191/share at the midpoint average of ORCL shares' historical trading premium at 20.54x EV/aEBITDA.

Placing a value on the firm, I believe ORCL shares should be priced at $271/share at 19.69x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending ORCL shares with a STRONG BUY.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.
